content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
David Rice 
Senior Staff Counsel 
State Water Resources Control Board 
P.O. Box 100 
Sacramento, CA 95812-0100 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-130 
Dear Mr. Rice: 
June 14,2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Daniel Selmi, a member of the 
Regional Water Quality Control Board, Santa Ana Region ("Santa Ana Water Board" or "Board") 
regarding conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. Also, the Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act 
as a finder of fact when rendering advice, and this letter is based on the facts presented. (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
May Santa Ana Water Board member Selmi participate in the Santa Ana Water Board's 
consideration and adoption of the Newport Bay Bacteria Amendment even though his residence is 
within 500 feet of Newport Bay, the area the amendment will regulate? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, because Mr. Selmi does not have a financial interest in the decision under the Act, he 
may participate. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are lo the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-130 
Page No. 2 
Mr. Selmi's residence is located less than 500 feet from Newport Bay, which is located 
along the coast in southern Orange County, California. Mr. Selmi is a member of the Santa Ana 
Water Board, an organization responsible for implementing, enforcing and adopting revisions to the 
Water Quality Control Plan for the Santa Ana River Basin ("Basin Plan").2 The Basin Plan is the 
central regulatory document which guides the protection of water quality within the Santa Ana 
region. The Basin Plan includes a list of waterbodies within the Board's jurisdiction, their beneficial 
uses, water quality objectives designed to protect those beneficial uses, and implementation 
strategies including the waterbody's pollutant total maximum daily loads ("TMDLs"3). The Santa 
Ana Water Board is considering the adoption of a "Newport Bay Bacteria Amendment" to revise 
the Basin Plan. 
The Newport Bay Bacteria Amendment to the Basin Plan: 
The Newport Bay Bacteria Amendment ("Bacteria Amendment") will amend the Basin Plan 
as follows: 
• revise compliance schedule requirements for an existing fecal coliform TMDL for shellfish 
harvesting (SHEL} in Newport Bay; 
• revise the SHEL beneficial use definition; 
• add Anti-Degradation Targets (water quality standards) for certain waters designated for 
non-primary contact recreation (REC2); 
• add new waterbodies to the Basin Plan; and designate beneficial uses for those waters as 
well as add beneficial uses for other waters already identified in the Basin Plan. 
The parts of the Bacteria Amendment which are most directly relevant to Newport Bay, and 
thus to Mr. Selmi's residence, are (1) the revision to the compliance schedule for the SHEL TMDL 
and (2) the revision to the SHEL beneficial use definition. 
(1) Shellfish Han1est Pol/want TMDLs Compliance Schedule Revision: The Bacteria 
Amendment proposes to extend the compliance schedule for complying with the SHEL TMDL 
from 2019 to 2022 to accommodate possible changes to bacteria indicators and associated water 
quality objectives for bacteria. These changes may impact how compliance with the SHEL TMDL 
is ultimately achieved. The extension of the compliance deadline itself will not impact water quality 
in Newport Bay because those responsible for implementing the SHEL TMDL will continue 
existing efforts to limit bacteria discharges and exceedances. 
(2) Shellfish Harvest Beneficial Use Definition Revision: The revision to the definition for 
SHEL is designed to create statewide consistency and is not expected to result in changes to 
existing protections for marine organisms. 
2 httn://www.waterboards.ca.gov/santaao.i/water issues/programs/basin plan/inde;s,shtm] 
3 TMDLs identify the maximum amount of a particular pollutant that a waterbody can absorb while 
maintaining protection of the associated beneficial uses. 


File No. A-17-130 
Page No. 3 
Other Revisions: Other portions of the Bacteria Amendment include the identification of 
new waterbodies in the Basin Plan, the designation of new beneficial uses for new and existing 
waterbodies in the Basin Plan, and the addition of Anti-Degradation Targets for REC2 waterbodies. 
These other revisions are believed to have at most marginal positive impacts on water quality in 
Newport Bay. Almost all of the waterbodies to be listed are already regulated by the Board pursuant 
to existing State and federal authority. Thus, listing these waterbodies acts to create consistency 
between the Basin Plan and the Board's existing regulatory activities. No additional water quality 
benefit, or adverse impact, is expected from the listing of these waterbodies because no additional 
regulatory requirements are expected following the adoption of the Bacteria Amendment. The same 
is true for the identification of additional beneficial uses for certain waterbodies. 
You note that most the waterbodies affected by Bacteria Amendment are in different 
municipal jurisdictions than the Selmi residence, and are located far from the residence. While 
many waterbodies within the Santa Ana region ultimately drain to Newport Bay and water quality 
improvements in upstream tributaries can positively impact water quality in Newport Bay, you state 
that any water quality improvements attributable to the Bacteria Amendment would be marginal 
and diluted by the time the waters reach Newport Bay. 
Adoption of the Bacteria Amendment will not result in any compliance actions directly on 
the Selmi residence. With the exception of the discharge prohibitions, the Basin Plan is not self­
executing and is not directly enforceable against persons or entities. Rather, the Board must first 
issue permits or waste discharge requirements imposing the specific requirements a discharger must 
meet. Only then are dischargers subject to enforcement for violating applicable requirements. The 
shellfish harvesting TMDL is implemented in the existing municipal separate storm sewer system 
permit for Orange County. The Board may consider reissuance of that permit later this year. 
Further, to the extent that the Bacteria Amendment requires additional efforts to protect newly 
identified waterbodies or existing waterbodies, none of these efforts would be imposed directly on 
Mr. Selmi's residence. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when a reasonably foreseeable financial effect 
of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material, and distinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally. 
Sectiol 87103 defines financial interests to include: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103( d) ). 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(b )), including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033). 


File No. A-17-130 
Page No. 4 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to 
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
Board member Selmi has a financial interest in his real property residence located within 
500 feet of Newport Bay. To determine ifhe has a prohibited conflict of interest under the Act we 
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect 
on his real property interest. (Regulation 18700(d).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is "explicitly" or 
"not explicitly" involved in a decision. (Regulation 18701.) Here, Mr. Selmi's real property is not 
explicitly involved in the decision, and the appropriate standard to determine foreseeability is 
"whether the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical 
or theoretical." (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
The standard for materiality is set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a), and includes those 
decisions which, as relevant here would do any of the following: 
(IO) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or 
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market 
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 
feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial 
property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed 
under Regulation 18702.1. 3 Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission 
may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these 
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property. 
The facts provided indicate that "any water quality improvements attributable to the 
Bacteria Amendment would be marginal and diluted by the time the waters reach Newport Bay." 
Therefore, it does not appear to be a realistic possibility that the Bacteria Amendment would have a 
financial effect on his residence. Additionally, based on the facts provided, the Bacteria 


File No. A-17-130 
Page No. 5 
Amendment would not change the character of the real property residence, nor cause a reasonably 
prudent person to determine the Amendment might impact the market value of his residence. The 
property is located within 500 feet of Newport Bay, the destination of the water carried in the 
waterbodies subject to the amendment. However, the facts indicate that the Bacteria Amendment 
will have a negligible impact on the Bay's water quality and the revisions ofrequirements will not 
impact Mr. Selmi's residence or his enjoyment thereof. The decision does not present a reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect, of a material nature, on his residence. Thus, Mr. Selmi does not have a 
financial interest in the decision under the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
Acting General Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
May Santa Ana Water Board member Selmi participate in the Santa Ana Water Board's 
consideration and adoption of the Newport Bay Bacteria Amendment even though his residence is 
within 500 feet of Newport Bay, the area the amendment will regulate?
sections.conclusion:
Yes, because Mr. Selmi does not have a financial interest in the decision under the Act, he 
may participate.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when a reasonably foreseeable financial effect 
of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material, and distinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally. 
Sectiol 87103 defines financial interests to include: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103( d) ). 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(b )), including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033).  
• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to 
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
Board member Selmi has a financial interest in his real property residence located within 
500 feet of Newport Bay. To determine ifhe has a prohibited conflict of interest under the Act we 
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect 
on his real property interest. (Regulation 18700(d).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is "explicitly" or 
"not explicitly" involved in a decision. (Regulation 18701.) Here, Mr. Selmi's real property is not 
explicitly involved in the decision, and the appropriate standard to determine foreseeability is 
"whether the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical 
or theoretical." (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
The standard for materiality is set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a), and includes those 
decisions which, as relevant here would do any of the following: 
(IO) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or 
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market 
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 
feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial 
property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed 
under Regulation 18702.1. 3 Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission 
may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these 
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property. 
The facts provided indicate that "any water quality improvements attributable to the 
Bacteria Amendment would be marginal and diluted by the time the waters reach Newport Bay." 
Therefore, it does not appear to be a realistic possibility that the Bacteria Amendment would have a 
financial effect on his residence. Additionally, based on the facts provided, the Bacteria  
Amendment would not change the character of the real property residence, nor cause a reasonably 
prudent person to determine the Amendment might impact the market value of his residence. The 
property is located within 500 feet of Newport Bay, the destination of the water carried in the 
waterbodies subject to the amendment. However, the facts indicate that the Bacteria Amendment 
will have a negligible impact on the Bay's water quality and the revisions ofrequirements will not 
impact Mr. Selmi's residence or his enjoyment thereof. The decision does not present a reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect, of a material nature, on his residence. Thus, Mr. Selmi does not have a 
financial interest in the decision under the Act.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Santa Ana Water Board member Selmi participate in the Santa Ana Water Board's 
consideration and adoption of the Newport Bay Bacteria Amendment even though his residence is 
within 500 feet of Newport Bay, the area the amendment will regulate?

CONCLUSION: Yes, because Mr. Selmi does not have a financial interest in the decision under the Act, he 
may participate.
